data.title: Is FedEx Stock Poised for Growth After Recent Earnings Rebound?
data.description:
If you’ve been eyeing FedEx stock and wondering if now is the time to make a move, you’re definitely not alone. With shares closing recently at $235.8, investors find themselves in the classic tug-of-war between value and momentum. Over the past week, the stock slipped by 1.7%, not a huge swing but just enough to make some folks cautious. In the past month, though, FedEx rebounded with a solid 4.5% gain, hinting that confidence in its long-term story might be reviving. Year to date, the stock is down 14.0%, and if we stretch out to a full year, it’s still off by 11.2%. But glance back three years and FedEx actually comes out well ahead, up an impressive 64.7%. This suggests resilience and underlying strength in the business even during volatile times.

Recent market chatter has highlighted shifting consumer behaviors and continued supply chain adaptation, both areas where FedEx naturally plays a lead role. These larger trends have caused some turbulence in shipping stocks, with adjustments in risk perception rippling through the sector. Another point of interest for investors is that FedEx comes out with a valuation score of 5 out of 6 on our undervaluation checklist. This means it passes nearly every test savvy investors look for when hunting for bargains in the market.

So, how exactly do we arrive at this score? Let’s dig into the valuation methods most investors rely on, and stick around until the end because I’ll share an even more insightful angle on understanding what FedEx might really be worth.

FedEx delivered -11.2% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Logistics industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: FedEx Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model helps estimate the true worth of a company by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today’s dollars. Essentially, it answers the question, “How much are FedEx’s expected future earnings worth right now?”

FedEx’s current Free Cash Flow sits at approximately $2.63 billion. Analyst forecasts stretch out over the next several years, with cash flows expected to rise steadily and reach about $7.1 billion by 2035. While detailed analyst estimates are available through 2028, projections beyond that are based on growth trends and industry estimates. All values are calculated in US dollars.

When running these numbers through the two-stage Free Cash Flow to Equity DCF model, FedEx’s intrinsic value comes out to $386.67 per share. This is 39.0% above its recent share price of $235.8, signaling the market might be significantly underestimating FedEx’s underlying cash-generating power.

In summary, the DCF approach suggests FedEx stock is trading well below its true value, offering a compelling case for investors looking for undervalued opportunities.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for FedEx.

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests FedEx is undervalued by 39.0%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover more undervalued stocks.

Approach 2: FedEx Price vs Earnings (PE Ratio)

The Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is a go-to valuation approach for companies like FedEx that are consistently profitable. This measure helps investors see how much they are paying for every dollar of the company's earnings, which is a crucial gauge when earnings are robust and recurring.

What counts as a “fair” PE can depend on growth prospects and perceived risks. Companies expected to deliver strong future growth can justify higher PE ratios, while those with slower growth or higher risks usually trade at lower multiples.

FedEx currently trades at a PE ratio of 13.5x. This is below both the industry average of 15.8x and the peer group average of 17.9x, indicating FedEx shares are priced more conservatively than its typical competitors.

To add another layer, Simply Wall St's "Fair Ratio" calculates what a truly appropriate multiple would be for FedEx. This proprietary metric weighs not just industry standards and peer groups, but also considers FedEx’s specific profit margins, growth outlook, size, and risk profile. Because of these tailored inputs, the Fair Ratio offers a more refined valuation benchmark than raw peer or industry averages alone.

For FedEx, the Fair Ratio stands at 17.7x, which is noticeably higher than its actual PE ratio of 13.5x. This gap suggests the market is assigning a lower value to FedEx’s earnings than what might be justified by its fundamentals.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your FedEx Narrative

Earlier we mentioned there’s an even better way to understand valuation, so let’s introduce you to Narratives. A Narrative is your story or perspective on a company; essentially, it’s how you connect what you know about FedEx with the numbers, by estimating what you think its future revenue, earnings, and fair value should be.

This approach links your view of FedEx’s real-world developments, strategies, and risks directly to fully quantified forecasts and a calculated fair value. This makes your investment decision more meaningful and personalized. Narratives are easy to use and available right on Simply Wall St’s Community page, where millions of investors share and debate their perspectives.

They give you a clear, visual way to see if your own fair value is above or below today’s price, making it easier to decide when to buy or sell. Since Narratives update dynamically when news or earnings reports are released, your analysis can keep up with the market.

For example, some investors see FedEx delivering aggressive margin improvements and set their Fair Value near $320, while others focus on ongoing economic and industry risks and feel it’s only worth $200. data.title: Are Investors Undervaluing SGS SA (VTX:SGSN) By 36%?
data.description:
Advertisement

Key Insights

  • SGS' estimated fair value is CHF137 based on 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity
  • SGS' CHF87.24 share price signals that it might be 36% undervalued
  • Our fair value estimate is 46% higher than SGS' analyst price target of CHF93.39

How far off is SGS SA (VTX:SGSN) from its intrinsic value? Using the most recent financial data, we'll take a look at whether the stock is fairly priced by estimating the company's future cash flows and discounting them to their present value. Our analysis will employ the Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model. Don't get put off by the jargon, the math behind it is actually quite straightforward.

We generally believe that a company's value is the present value of all of the cash it will generate in the future. However, a DCF is just one valuation metric among many, and it is not without flaws. If you still have some burning questions about this type of valuation, take a look at the Simply Wall St analysis model.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Crunching The Numbers

We're using the 2-stage growth model, which simply means we take in account two stages of company's growth. In the initial period the company may have a higher growth rate and the second stage is usually assumed to have a stable growth rate. In the first stage we need to estimate the cash flows to the business over the next ten years. Where possible we use analyst estimates, but when these aren't available we extrapolate the previous free cash flow (FCF) from the last estimate or reported value. We assume companies with shrinking free cash flow will slow their rate of shrinkage, and that companies with growing free cash flow will see their growth rate slow, over this period. We do this to reflect that growth tends to slow more in the early years than it does in later years.

Generally we assume that a dollar today is more valuable than a dollar in the future, so we need to discount the sum of these future cash flows to arrive at a present value estimate:

10-year free cash flow (FCF) estimate

2026202720282029203020312032203320342035
Levered FCF (CHF, Millions) CHF881.3mCHF954.2mCHF978.0mCHF1.03bCHF1.06bCHF1.08bCHF1.10bCHF1.12bCHF1.13bCHF1.14b
Growth Rate Estimate SourceAnalyst x6Analyst x6Analyst x2Analyst x1Est @ 3.16%Est @ 2.35%Est @ 1.78%Est @ 1.38%Est @ 1.10%Est @ 0.91%
Present Value (CHF, Millions) Discounted @ 4.5% CHF843CHF874CHF857CHF860CHF849CHF832CHF810CHF786CHF760CHF734

("Est" = FCF growth rate estimated by Simply Wall St)
Present Value of 10-year Cash Flow (PVCF) = CHF8.2b

The second stage is also known as Terminal Value, this is the business's cash flow after the first stage. For a number of reasons a very conservative growth rate is used that cannot exceed that of a country's GDP growth. In this case we have used the 5-year average of the 10-year government bond yield (0.4%) to estimate future growth. In the same way as with the 10-year 'growth' period, we discount future cash flows to today's value, using a cost of equity of 4.5%.

Terminal Value (TV)= FCF2035 × (1 + g) ÷ (r – g) = CHF1.1b× (1 + 0.4%) ÷ (4.5%– 0.4%) = CHF28b

Present Value of Terminal Value (PVTV)= TV / (1 + r)10= CHF28b÷ ( 1 + 4.5%)10= CHF18b

The total value is the sum of cash flows for the next ten years plus the discounted terminal value, which results in the Total Equity Value, which in this case is CHF26b. The last step is to then divide the equity value by the number of shares outstanding. Compared to the current share price of CHF87.2, the company appears quite good value at a 36% discount to where the stock price trades currently. The assumptions in any calculation have a big impact on the valuation, so it is better to view this as a rough estimate, not precise down to the last cent.

The Assumptions

We would point out that the most important inputs to a discounted cash flow are the discount rate and of course the actual cash flows. If you don't agree with these result, have a go at the calculation yourself and play with the assumptions. The DCF also does not consider the possible cyclicality of an industry, or a company's future capital requirements, so it does not give a full picture of a company's potential performance. Given that we are looking at SGS as potential shareholders, the cost of equity is used as the discount rate, rather than the cost of capital (or weighted average cost of capital, WACC) which accounts for debt. In this calculation we've used 4.5%, which is based on a levered beta of 0.959. Beta is a measure of a stock's volatility, compared to the market as a whole. We get our beta from the industry average beta of globally comparable companies, with an imposed limit between 0.8 and 2.0, which is a reasonable range for a stable business.

Check out our latest analysis for SGS

SWOT Analysis for SGS

Strength
  • Earnings growth over the past year exceeded the industry.
  • Debt is well covered by earnings and cashflows.
    Balance sheet summary for SGSN.
Weakness
  • Dividend is low compared to the top 25% of dividend payers in the Professional Services market.
Opportunity
  • Annual earnings are forecast to grow faster than the Swiss market.
  • Trading below our estimate of fair value by more than 20%.
Threat
  • Dividends are not covered by earnings.
  • Revenue is forecast to grow slower than 20% per year.
    See SGSN's dividend history.

Next Steps:

Valuation is only one side of the coin in terms of building your investment thesis, and it is only one of many factors that you need to assess for a company. It's not possible to obtain a foolproof valuation with a DCF model. Rather it should be seen as a guide to "what assumptions need to be true for this stock to be under/overvalued?" For example, changes in the company's cost of equity or the risk free rate can significantly impact the valuation. What is the reason for the share price sitting below the intrinsic value? For SGS, there are three additional items you should assess:

  1. Risks: Take risks, for example - SGS has 2 warning signs we think you should be aware of.
  2. Future Earnings: How does SGSN's growth rate compare to its peers and the wider market? Dig deeper into the analyst consensus number for the upcoming years by interacting with our free analyst growth expectation chart.

Despite an already strong run, Catering International & Services Société Anonyme (EPA:ALCIS) shares have been powering on, with a gain of 27% in the last thirty days. The last 30 days bring the annual gain to a very sharp 48%.

Even after such a large jump in price, Catering International & Services Société Anonyme's price-to-earnings (or "P/E") ratio of 14x might still make it look like a buy right now compared to the market in France, where around half of the companies have P/E ratios above 17x and even P/E's above 34x are quite common. Although, it's not wise to just take the P/E at face value as there may be an explanation why it's limited.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

With its earnings growth in positive territory compared to the declining earnings of most other companies, Catering International & Services Société Anonyme has been doing quite well of late. It might be that many expect the strong earnings performance to degrade substantially, possibly more than the market, which has repressed the P/E. If not, then existing shareholders have reason to be quite optimistic about the future direction of the share price.

Check out our latest analysis for Catering International & Services Société Anonyme

Want the full picture on analyst estimates for the company? Then our free report on Catering International & Services Société Anonyme will help you uncover what's on the horizon.
Advertisement

What Are Growth Metrics Telling Us About The Low P/E?

The only time you'd be truly comfortable seeing a P/E as low as Catering International & Services Société Anonyme's is when the company's growth is on track to lag the market.

Taking a look back first, we see that the company grew earnings per share by an impressive 173% last year. Still, incredibly EPS has fallen 19% in total from three years ago, which is quite disappointing. So unfortunately, we have to acknowledge that the company has not done a great job of growing earnings over that time.

Shifting to the future, estimates from the only analyst covering the company suggest earnings should grow by 18% each year over the next three years. With the market only predicted to deliver 12% per year, the company is positioned for a stronger earnings result.

In light of this, it's peculiar that Catering International & Services Société Anonyme's P/E sits below the majority of other companies. It looks like most investors are not convinced at all that the company can achieve future growth expectations.

The Key Takeaway

The latest share price surge wasn't enough to lift Catering International & Services Société Anonyme's P/E close to the market median. We'd say the price-to-earnings ratio's power isn't primarily as a valuation instrument but rather to gauge current investor sentiment and future expectations.

Our examination of Catering International & Services Société Anonyme's analyst forecasts revealed that its superior earnings outlook isn't contributing to its P/E anywhere near as much as we would have predicted. When we see a strong earnings outlook with faster-than-market growth, we assume potential risks are what might be placing significant pressure on the P/E ratio. At least price risks look to be very low, but investors seem to think future earnings could see a lot of volatility.

Price-to-Book Ratio of 5x: Is it justified?

Viking Therapeutics currently trades on a price-to-book ratio of 5x, presenting a noticeably different valuation compared to its sector peers. At a last close of $35.12, this multiple signals a significant valuation premium and prompts questions about how the market is weighing future potential.

The price-to-book ratio compares the market value of the company to its book value and is often used for biotech stocks that have volatile or negative earnings. In the case of Viking, this metric is relevant given its unprofitable status and primarily asset-driven value proposition.

While Viking's price-to-book ratio of 5x appears reasonable compared to the peer average of 12x, it is considered expensive when measured against the broader US Biotechs industry average of 2.6x. This suggests that, despite peer-relative value, the broader market may remain skeptical of Viking's near-term profitability. There is currently insufficient data to determine what a "fair" price-to-book ratio should be for Viking, so the market's premium appears to rest on expectations for future success rather than proven profitability.
